steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student claims that some modern southwestern irrigation methods have a lineage that long predates the Canyon Creek Reservoir's 1900 completion. The evidence must show that today's methods descend from or still use principles developed well before 1900.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — "In the decades following the Second World War, irrigation of arid areas for agriculture increased to such a degree that it now accounts for roughly 70% of the world's water usage.": Post-WWII is the 1940s–onward — far too recent to predate 1900. No historical lineage is established. ✗
  • Choice B — "The importance of the development of irrigation infrastructure in the American Southwest today cannot be overstated, since it is the most common means of conveying water for food production.": Speaks only to present-day importance; says nothing about the age or origin of current methods. ✗
  • Choice C — "Sprinkler irrigation systems, which were developed in the late 20th century, are a convenient technique of irrigating that makes use of machinery in order to spray water in all directions. Many areas of the Southwestern United States are currently irrigated by this method.": Explicitly places the origin in the late 20th century — after 1900, the opposite of what the claim needs. ✗
  • Choice D — "The irrigation system developed by the Hohokam people in what is now central Arizona in the 7th century CE was simple but made use of hydraulic engineering design principles that are still utilized by today's engineers.": The 7th century CE is hundreds of years before 1900, and the phrase "still utilized by today's engineers" directly links that ancient lineage to modern practice. ✓
That matches Choice D — "The irrigation system developed by the Hohokam people in what is now central Arizona in the 7th century CE was simple but made use of hydraulic engineering design principles that are still utilized by today's engineers.".
